topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat. The headline, the carefully crafted prose, the entire intellectual architecture of her recent essay—it was all attributed to Dr. Alistair Finch. A cold, heavy sensation settled in her chest, squeezing the air from her lungs. She blinked, slowly, as if trying to clear a fog. Her hands, which had been gripping the mouse so tightly her knuckles were white, began to tremble. She reread the byline, then the accompanying text, her eyes darting back and forth, searching for a misplaced comma, a telltale sign of error. Nothing. It was undeniably Finch’s work, hers stolen, published under a borrowed identity. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped her lips, a ragged little puff of air. She leaned back in her chair, the plastic groaning under her weight, and for a long moment, she just sat there, utterly still, processing the sheer impossibility of the situation.

[story 2]

"No. No, this can't be right." Marcus muttered, the words barely audible over the hum of the office air conditioning. He picked up the printed proof, his fingers tracing the unfamiliar signature at the bottom. His jaw tightened, the muscles standing out sharply beneath his skin. He shook the paper lightly, a nervous, jerky motion. He looked up at Sarah, his eyes wide and unblinking. "Look at this. The byline. It says Alistair Finch." A slow, creeping dread began to coil in his stomach, tightening with every passing second. He felt a sudden, dizzying lurch, as if the floor had tilted beneath him. He pushed the papers away, the movement stiff and unnatural, and took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to force his racing heart to slow down.

[story 3]

The realization hit Liam like a physical blow. He sat bolt upright, the cheap chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. His vision swam momentarily, the bright office lights blurring into streaks of white. He stared at the published piece, the familiar arguments and nuanced observations now seemingly belonging to another man. His mouth went dry, and he swallowed hard, the movement jerky. He brought a hand up, pressing it against his forehead as if to ward off a sudden fever. A strange, hollow feeling washed over him, leaving him feeling strangely disconnected from the room. He slowly lowered his hand, his gaze fixed on the name, the letters seeming to mock him with their false authority.
